summary: I was born and raised in Sacramento, California, where my family ran a fish market. I went to Sacramento Junior College and took art courses there alongside classmates like Mark Mizuguchi and Dick Kurihara. Before the war, Sacramento was home, but after evacuation, I never returned because I moved east to New York to find work.

Mark Mizuguchi was one of my classmates in the art course at Sacramento Junior College. We studied together before the war disrupted our lives. I remember him as part of the circle of friends I had during those early years in Sacramento.

Dick Kurihara was a close friend and classmate of mine in Sacramento. Our families were connected since his father worked as a bookkeeper for my father's fish market. Dick was a couple of years older but we shared similar experiences, including attending the same high school and facing the upheaval of evacuation during the war.

My oldest sister went to Los Angeles around age 17 to pursue a singing career and tried to get a recording contract with Columbia Records. Although she didn’t get a recording deal there, she started her singing career in Japan. Los Angeles was a stepping stone for her ambitions in music.

My sister attempted to get a recording contract with Columbia Records in Los Angeles when she was about 17. Although she never secured a deal, she was connected enough to know Ella Fitzgerald and later pursued a singing career in Japan. Columbia Records represented a goal she reached for in her early music ambitions.

My sister knew Ella Fitzgerald during her time in Los Angeles when she was trying to get a recording contract. Although she never recorded with Columbia Records, her connection to Ella Fitzgerald shows the circles she was involved in as she pursued singing. It was part of her early music aspirations before she moved to Japan.

Japan was where my oldest sister was raised by our grandparents before coming to California for high school. After starting her singing career in the U.S., she eventually returned to Japan to continue it. Also, my parents were originally from Japan, and my father came to the U.S. as a young man.

After the assembly center, I was sent by train to the Tule Lake War Relocation Center during the Japanese American internment. There, I worked on the Tulean Dispatch newspaper’s art staff, doing layout and lettering. Life was focused on work and routine, and I didn’t participate much in recreational activities.

During World War II, I was interned as part of the Japanese American internment, first at an assembly center and then at Tule Lake. We were forced to evacuate Sacramento, and my family lost our fish market equipment. The internment was a difficult time, but I answered 'yes/yes' on the loyalty questionnaire, which caused tension with my father.

Mr. Sakayama was the block manager at Tule Lake and had a son who was two years older than me. He advised his son to answer 'no/no' on the loyalty questionnaire, and my father was influenced by this stance. This led to a disagreement between my father and me when I answered 'yes/yes' instead.

At Tule Lake, I worked for the Tulean Dispatch newspaper as part of the art staff. We handled everything from cutting headlines to tracing lettering on mimeograph machines. It was an all-Japanese run paper with separate English and Japanese sections, and I focused on my art work there.

After leaving the internment camp, I stayed at a Quaker hostel in Cincinnati while looking for a job. They provided board and room at no charge, and I did housework in exchange. The Quakers helped many of us transition out of camp life and into civilian society.

I eventually moved to New York City after leaving the Quaker hostel in Cincinnati. There, I found work at Famous Studios, an animation cartoon studio that had just relocated to New York. It was a fresh start for me in the art field, even though I began at the lowest level doing in-betweening.

At Famous Studios in New York, I started as an in-betweener, drawing frames between key animations for cartoons. They made characters like Little Lulu and Popeye the Sailor. Because of my art background, I was promoted to rendering background scenes, which paid better and suited my skills more.

Little Lulu was one of the cartoon characters produced by Famous Studios where I worked in New York. I contributed by doing background rendering for these cartoons after starting as an in-betweener. It was part of my early career in animation before I was drafted.

Popeye the Sailor was another cartoon character made by Famous Studios during my time there. I worked on the animation by rendering backgrounds for these cartoons. It was a valuable experience in the art and animation field before military service.

I was drafted into the United States Army during World War II after working in New York. I trained in Mississippi and was later sent to Fort Snelling for Military Intelligence training because of my Japanese language skills. After training, I was deployed to the Philippines and then to Tokyo for translation work.

During basic training in Mississippi, I was lined up to be a replacement for the 442nd Infantry Regiment, a unit composed mostly of Japanese American soldiers. However, I was selected for Military Intelligence and sent to Fort Snelling instead. I knew some friends who served with the 442nd and were wounded or killed in Europe.

Fort Snelling in Minnesota was where I completed my Military Intelligence training after basic training. It was there that I had a three-day pass and met Martha and her cousin Yoshiko in Chicago. Fort Snelling was a key step before my deployment overseas.

I went to Mississippi for my basic training in the Army during World War II. After finishing basic, I was slated to join the 442nd Infantry Regiment but was reassigned to Fort Snelling for Military Intelligence. Mississippi was where my military journey began.

While on a pass from Fort Snelling, I went to Chicago to visit Martha, a fellow art classmate. Although she was sick with mumps, I ended up going out with her cousin Yoshiko, who later became my wife through correspondence while I was overseas. Chicago was where a significant personal relationship began.

I was deployed to the Philippines with the Army during World War II. Shortly after arriving, the war with Japan ended. From there, I was sent to Tokyo to work in Military Intelligence translating newspapers.

World War II drastically changed my life, from being interned at Tule Lake to being drafted into the Army. I trained in Mississippi and Fort Snelling, served in the Philippines, and then worked in Tokyo after the war ended. The war interrupted my art career but also led to new experiences and relationships.

After the war ended, I was stationed in Tokyo at the Dai-Ichi Life Insurance Company building working for Military Intelligence translating newspapers. I was able to locate my sister there, who was a well-known singer performing with Dick Mine's band. Tokyo was where I reunited with family after years apart.

In Tokyo, I was stationed at the Dai-Ichi Life Insurance Company building as part of my Military Intelligence duties translating newspapers. It was near a police station where I was able to arrange a visit with my sister, who was performing there. The building was my base during the occupation period.

Dick Mine was a musician whose band my sister sang with during her career in Japan. When I found my sister in Tokyo after the war, she was performing with Dick Mine’s band. It was reassuring to know she was safe and continuing her music despite the war.

Although not explicitly named in my story, my work translating newspapers in Tokyo was part of the Military Intelligence efforts, which likely connected to the Allied Translator and Interpreter Section. My Japanese language skills from school helped me serve in this capacity after the war.

The First Cavalry Division was not directly mentioned in my experience, as I was assigned to Military Intelligence after basic training. My military service focused on translation and intelligence rather than cavalry operations.

My mother was from Hiratsuka, Kanagawa, Japan, and my father came from the same area. This is where our family roots are, and although I was born in Sacramento, our heritage traces back to Hiratsuka. It was important in shaping our family’s identity and connections.
